era_context: The mid-second millennium BCE, with the Hittite Empire expanding under Mursili I, Mycenaean Greece developing palace societies, the Shang Dynasty consolidating in China, and Egypt's New Kingdom establishing itself as a major power. This period is characterized by the development of complex administrative systems, long-distance trade networks, and the refinement of bronze metallurgy across multiple civilizations.

events.title: Possible volcanic eruption or climate event
events.description: Some scholars have proposed that a major volcanic eruption occurred in the Aegean around this time period, though the exact dating is highly disputed. If such an eruption occurred, it would have had significant impacts on Mediterranean trade networks and climate. However, the dating of the Theran eruption remains one of the most contentious issues in Bronze Age chronology, with proposals ranging from 1650 to 1500 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The dating of the Theran eruption is highly uncertain, with radiocarbon dating, ice core analysis, and archaeological stratigraphy producing conflicting results. The eruption may have occurred 50–100 years earlier or later than this date.

disconfirming_evidence: The Theran eruption dating is one of the most disputed topics in Bronze Age studies. Radiocarbon dating suggests 1650–1600 BCE, but some scholars argue for 1550–1500 BCE based on archaeological correlations. The eruption's impact on the Minoan civilization is also debated; some scholars argue the Minoan collapse was caused by the eruption, while others contend it occurred decades later and was caused by other factors (invasion, internal conflict). Including this event at all is speculative; it may not have occurred in 1628 BCE or even in this decade.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially undocumented in contemporary written sources. The only potential event is geological/environmental, and even that is highly speculative. The historical record for 1628 BCE is negligible. Any narrative about this year must rely on archaeological inference and geological evidence, both of which are subject to significant dating uncertainty.
